perturbation.original_text: 3. KEEPING CONFIDENT IAL INFORMATION C ONFIDENTIAL Each party shall in relation to the Confidential Information of the other party exercise no lesser security measures and degree of care than those which it applies to its own confidential information and which it warrants as providing adequate protection against any unauthorised disclosure, copying or use.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. KEEPING CONFIDENT IAL INFORMATION C ONFIDENTIAL Each party shall in relation to the Confidential Information of the other party exercise security measures and degree of care that are standard in the industry, but are not required to be the same as those applied to its own confidential information. Each party warrants that the security measures and degree of care applied are commercially reasonable, but does not guarantee complete protection against unauthorised disclosure, copying or use.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by weakening the standard of care required to protect confidential information. It changes the standard from exercising 'no lesser security measures and degree of care' than those applied to its own confidential information, to exercising security measures that are 'standard in the industry' and 'commercially reasonable'. However, the original wording of 3 requires adequate protection, which is very different from commercially reasonable. This creates an in-text contradiction with the obligations under clause 2.1. It is now unclear what level of protection is required.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 to keep the other party's Confidential Information confidential including, without limitation, taking the measures set out in Clause 3;
perturbation.justification: The original text required 'no lesser security measures' than those applied to its own confidential information, warranting 'adequate protection'. The changed text weakens this standard, requiring only 'standard in the industry' and 'commercially reasonable' measures, without guaranteeing 'complete protection'. This contradicts clause 2.1, which requires taking the measures in Clause 3 to 'keep the other party's Confidential Information confidential'. The changed text's lower standard of care directly conflicts with the original requirement for 'adequate protection' and the obligation to keep information confidential, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. EXCEPTIONS This Agreement does not apply to Confidential Information: 5.1 to the extent that the Confidential Information is or comes into the public domain other than by breach of this Agreement; 5.2 which the Receiving Party can show by its written or other records was in its possession prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party and which had not previously been obtained from the Disclosing Party or another person under an obligation of confiden tiality ; 5.3 which the Receiving Party obtains or has avai lable from a source other than the Disclosing Party without breaching any obligation of confiden tiality ; 5.4 which has been disclosed by the Disclosing Party to another person without an obligation of confiden tiality .
perturbation.changed_text: 5. EXCEPTIONS This Agreement does not apply to Confidential Information: 5.1 to the extent that the Confidential Information is or comes into the public domain other than by breach of this Agreement; 5.2 which the Receiving Party can show by its written or other records was in its possession prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party and which had not previously been obtained from the Disclosing Party or another person under an obligation of confiden tiality ; 5.3 which the Receiving Party obtains or has avai lable from a source other than the Disclosing Party without breaching any obligation of confiden tiality ; 5.4 which has been disclosed by the Disclosing Party to another person without an obligation of confiden tiality . Notwithstanding the foregoing, if Confidential Information becomes publicly available due to a security breach on the Receiving Party's systems, that information will still be considered Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by adding that if Confidential Information becomes publicly available due to a security breach on the Receiving Party's systems, that information will still be considered Confidential Information. This conflicts with the clause 5.1 which states this agreement does not apply to Confidential Information to the extent that the Confidential Information is or comes into the public domain other than by breach of this Agreement. This creates uncertainty as to whether the receiving party is still responsible for information that is in the public domain. It now becomes unclear if 5.1 is valid
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.1 to the extent that the Confidential Information is or comes into the public domain other than by breach of this Agreement;
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a condition that Confidential Information remains confidential even if it becomes publicly available due to a security breach on the Receiving Party's systems. This directly contradicts section 5.1, which states that the agreement does not apply to Confidential Information that comes into the public domain. The change creates a clear inconsistency as to whether information in the public domain is still considered confidential under specific circumstances, creating a strong in-text contradiction as per the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 8. TERM AND TERMINATION 8.1 This Agreement shall come into effect (i) until parties sign the underlying documentation in relation to the Project, or (ii) in the event parties do not sign any such documentation , on the date of this Agreement and shall continue to be in full force and effect for a period of 2 years.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. TERM AND TERMINATION 8.1 This Agreement shall come into effect (i) until parties sign the underlying documentation in relation to the Project, or (ii) in the event parties do not sign any such documentation , on the date of this Agreement and shall continue to be in full force and effect for a period of 2 years, unless either party provides 30 days' written notice of termination. The obligations of confidentiality under this agreement shall survive termination indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction. While the original clause 8.1 states the agreement is in full force and effect for 2 years, the added text allows either party to terminate the agreement with 30 days' written notice. This contradicts the fixed 2-year term. The clause 'The obligations of confidentiality under this agreement shall survive termination indefinitely' creates further ambiguity, particularly conflicting with clause 8.2, in this change. The text now can be terminated before the 2 year period is over.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.2 Each party undertakes within 14 days of receipt of a wri tten request of the other party: 8.2.1 subject to Clause 8.3, to return to the other party all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control that contain any part of the other party's Confidential Information; or 8.2.2 subject to Clause 8.3, to de stroy by shredding or incineration all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control which reflect or have - 4 - been generated from any part of the other party's Confidential Information and to certify to the other party that this has been done.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an in-text contradiction because it allows for termination with 30 days' notice, contradicting the original agreement which stated a fixed term of 2 years as per clause 8.1. This directly conflicts with the obligations outlined in clause 8.2, which implicitly assumes the contract will remain in effect for the full 2-year term, particularly concerning the return or destruction of confidential information, as the original text makes no exceptions for early termination.

perturbation.original_text: 10.3 A person who is not a party to this Agreemen t has no right under section 6:253 of the Dutch Civil Code to enforce or to enjoy the benefit of any term of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.3 A person who is not a party to this Agreemen t has no right under section 6:253 of the Dutch Civil Code to enforce or to enjoy the benefit of any term of this Agreement. Notwithstanding the foregoing, FMO's investors may enforce the terms of this agreement as third-party beneficiaries.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by stating that 'FMO's investors may enforce the terms of this agreement as third-party beneficiaries.' This directly contradicts the previous sentence which states 'A person who is not a party to this Agreement has no right under section 6:253 of the Dutch Civil Code to enforce or to enjoy the benefit of any term of this Agreement.' It creates uncertainty about who can enforce the agreement and what rights third parties have. This is further confusing and contradicts with the jurisdiction section 11.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11. GOVERNING LAW AND JU RISDICTION This Agreement and any non -contractual obligations arising out of or in connection with it shall be governed by the laws of The Netherlands and the competent court in The Hague, The Netherlands shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute which may arise from or in connection with it.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds an exception allowing FMO's investors to enforce the agreement as third-party beneficiaries, which directly contradicts the original statement in section 10.3 that no one who is not a party to the agreement has the right to enforce any term. This also potentially contradicts section 11 by introducing uncertainty about jurisdiction, as FMO investors are granted enforcement rights outside of the original parties to the contract, which could complicate the governing law and jurisdiction stated in section 11.
